title: Synthetic Data
description: Training material produced or transformed by one model to teach another, distinct from raw web corpora collected for pretraining or demonstrations written directly by people.
openingSummary: Synthetic data is model-generated training material—examples, labels, traces, or preference judgments created by one model to teach another—not a catch-all label for every dataset used in machine learning.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: Synthetic data is training material produced or transformed by a model so another model can learn from it. A team might ask a teacher model to write question-answer pairs, grade student answers, record step-by-step reasoning traces, or compare two candidate replies. The defining trait is authorship path: the training signal was generated for this recipe rather than scraped from existing public pages or typed directly by human annotators for that step.

whyItMatters.title: Why It Matters
whyItMatters.body: Synthetic data lets teams scale coverage into rare scenarios, languages, or task formats without paying humans to write every example. It can also lower the cost of labels and preference comparisons when a strong teacher model is already available. The tradeoff is quality inheritance: the student learns whatever habits, blind spots, and biases the generator encodes, so synthetic pipelines need filtering, verification, and periodic refresh.

simpleExample.title: Simple Example
simpleExample.body: A product team needs a small assistant model for internal documentation search but has only a handful of human-written demos. They prompt a larger teacher model to draft hundreds of realistic user questions with grounded answers drawn from the company's docs, review a sample for obvious errors, and fine-tune the student on the accepted synthetic set.

commonConfusions.title: Common Confusions
commonConfusions.body: Synthetic data is not a synonym for all training data. Web pretraining corpora are collected from public text that already existed online; they are not produced specifically as a training signal for the target recipe. Human-authored instruction data is written directly by people who demonstrate the desired behavior. Synthetic data sits in between: a model creates or transforms the supervision signal that the training step consumes.
